Biography
Dr. Robert Lightfoot holds a BS in Computer Science and a PhD in
Multidisciplinary Engineering (combining computer science and engineering
education), both from Texas A&M University. He also earned an MS in
Software Engineering from Southern Methodist University. With 28 years of
industry experience, Dr. Lightfoot has developed large-scale networks and
network-controlled robotics, bringing real-world expertise to the classroom.
Socials
AOL, MySpace

chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content:
Cloud Computing. (3-0). Credit 3. Operating system and distributed systems fields
that form the basis of cloud computing such as virtualization, key-value storage
solutions, group membership, failure detection, peer to peer systems, datacenter
networking, resource management and scalability; popular frameworks such as
MapReduce and HDFS and case studies on failure determination. Prerequisite:
Grade of C or better in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331.

chunks.title: Additional Course Details
chunks.content:
Fundamentals of cloud computing, principles of virtualization, virtual machines, identity and
access management, virtual storage and networking, virtual private cloud, relational and non-
relational database services in cloud, security, monitoring, cost management, high availability and
scaling, serverless and containers in cloud.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
At the conclusion of this course, students should be able to:
• Discuss the concepts of cloud computing
• Utilize key underlying technologies such as virtualization
• Design and build simple architectures in cloud
• Monitor and manage lifecycle of virtual entities in cloud using tooling from a major cloud
provider
• Produce highly available, elastic and resilient systems using cloud architectural patterns
• Analyze problems and solutions to become an entry level cloud practitioner

chunks.title: Additional Instructional Materials
chunks.content:
This Material is: Required
Perusall
Notes:
We will use Perusall for our reading this semester. This will allow discussion and questions about
the content and server as a class library for documents we will read and discuss.
Textbook: Cloud Computing Sehgal-Bhatt 2020 Second edition.
We will also reference NIST Cloud Computing Documents.
1. The link to Perusall will be available in Canvas and connect you to the correct
course.
2. You must click each and all assignments in Canvas to get credit for the
reading.
We will use real world cloud services. On average, students spend less than $4.00 on these
services. For example, I leave my CSCE project 4 and 5 running all year and I am charged $0.56
per month.
You will need a pen or pencil for this class as well.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
• Grades are based on a weighted average. The letter grade will be assigned
on the %Total.
○ Total >= 90 A
○ 80 <= Total < 90 B
○ 70 <= Total < 80_C
○ 60 <= Total < 70 D
○ Total < 60 F
•
This course will allow you to show the achievement of the learning
outcomes through the following methods:
• Exams 30%
■ Midterm 10%
Final 20%
• Quizzes 5%
• Discussions 5%
• Readings 15%
Perusall 10%
Knowledge Checks 5%
• Projects 45%
Virtualization 5%
■ Multi VM setup 5%
Platform 15%
■ AWS config – https 5%
■ CI/CD 15%
• For any in-class activity, you must be in class to participate or receive credit.
This includes quizzes, discussions, and exams. To ensure excellence in this
course, you must pass (greater than 60%) each of the above categories.
(Below 60 in any category will result in a letter grade reduction for the
course.)
• These grades represent varying degrees of achievement (see the university's
grading system):
• A = excellent, B = good, C = satisfactory, D = passing, F = failing
• For any submission for an assignment, the work must meet a minimum viable
solution to the assignment. Failure to submit this will result in a zero grade.
• Rubrics will be used for instructors to apply consistent grading throughout
the class. They may be changed during the grading process as needed.
Rubrics may not be available while you are completing the assignment. Use
the assignment to understand the proposed problem.
You must develop your projects using a private repository on GitHub, where you will be part of an
organization. Keep your repository private. Your repository is a documentation of your
development process. You must have clear commit messages documenting the bugs you
identified, corresponding fixes, addition of test cases, etc. Your GitHub usage will impact your
grade. For example, having all the code added to the repository and submitted within a short
period (the magic of going from no code to a working solution quickly) will result in a very low
grade.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Knowledge checks, quizzes, and reading are not accepted late without a university-excused
absence.
Other assignments will be accepted up to 24 hours after the deadline with a 30% penalty.
• Late work will be accepted without penalty on the first class day after the
University reopens after closing for an unexpected event. (The newest
SARS, Snow Day, Hurricane Day, etc.)
• Late work will not be accepted for full credit for planned events. If you are
participating in a university-sponsored event, have your work done prior to
the due date. This includes interviews, conferences, etc.
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late
work and is exempted from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
With the emergence of artificial intelligence (AI) technologies, the ways in which we define our
creative processes continue to transform. Al generators are rapidly evolving from simple editing
for grammatical errors and spelling mistakes (Grammarly, MS Word Spell Check) to sophisticated
text production (ChatGPT, Google Bard, etc.), as well as image, computer code, and audio
generation. The presence of such tools, however, does not replace our need to learn how to
draft, revise, and reflect on texts, programs, drawings and how to exercise information literacy
and personal responsibility in how we locate, evaluate, incorporate, and cite primary/ secondary
sources. For example, the Association for Writing Across the Curriculum states the following:
Writing to learn is an intellectual activity that is crucial to the cognitive and social development of
learners and writers. This vital activity cannot be replaced by Al language generators (AWAC).
Engaging in the various aspects of creative pursuits (e.g., writing, coding, drawing) is critical to
education in a broad sense. While Al technologies will continue shaping how we approach these
creative tasks, the critical work of creativity relies on integrity, originality, and ethical conduct in
regard to appropriate representation as an author or creator. Thus, submitting work with a
significant percentage of Al-generated content, unless otherwise permitted, can be considered
academic misconduct under Texas A&M University Student Rule 20. Students must therefore cite
the use of Generative Al tools and document what they have contributed to an assignment.
